Design & Development Internship — Dune Buggy 4×2

Concept
to
Prototype

A documentation of my internship where I was entrusted with the complete design of a two-seater 4×2 Dune Buggy — from market research and initial sketches all the way to two fully-developed CAD prototypes, all delivered within one month.

1
Month
2
Prototypes
10"
Wheel Travel
Scroll
01 — The Project

END to END
development of a Custom
OFF ROAD architecture

I was entrusted with the complete design of a two-seater 4×2 Dune Buggy powered by a 250cc KTM Duke engine with chain drive. The brief demanded an off-road capable vehicle with a proper double wishbone front suspension setup — designed from the ground up, not adapted from an existing platform.

My scope encompassed the full product development lifecycle: from initial market research and reverse engineering to ergonomic packaging in CATIA and multi-body dynamics in Lotus Shark. A critical phase involved the parallel development of two distinct roll cage configurations. By conducting a comparative load path analysis, I down-selected a final architecture that optimized the strength-to-weight ratio while significantly reducing manufacturing complexity—all within an accelerated one-month delivery window.

SolidWorks CAD
CATIA Ergonomics
Suspension Design
Lotus Shark

Vehicle Type
Two-seater 4×2 Dune Buggy · Off-road recreational / competition
Powertrain
250cc KTM Duke Engine · Chain Drive · Rear-wheel drive
Suspension Spec
Double wishbone front · 10 inches wheel travel · Minimal camber gain · Damper optimised on lower arm
Deliverable
Comprehensive design package delivered in 1 month, including:
  • • Dual high-fidelity CAD roll cage concepts (Iterations A and B)
  • • Load path and structural efficiency evaluation
  • • Final down-selection documentation with rationalization for complexity reduction and material optimization

04 — What I Delivered
🔧
Technical Achievement

Suspension Geometry Mastery

Used Lotus Shark to iterate double wishbone geometry until camber gain across 10 inches of travel was minimised. Damper position on the lower arm was validated for optimal motion ratio — not placed by gut feel.

⚡
Process Achievement

Full Cycle in 30 Days

Market research to final prototype architecture development within a single month demonstrated the ability to manage parallel design workstreams — CATIA ergonomic packaging, Lotus Shark suspension kinematic optimisation, and SolidWorks chassis development all progressing simultaneously while early roll cage concepts were evaluated and converged into a structurally efficient final design.

05 — Reflections

What
I learned.

A month of complete ownership is a different kind of education. These are the four things that stuck.

01
Suspension geometry is never obvious — simulate first

My initial double wishbone geometry looked correct on paper but produced excessive camber gain at full bump. Only Lotus Shark revealed the issue before it was committed to CAD. The lesson: analysis tools aren't a validation step at the end — they're a design tool throughout. Simulate early, iterate often.

02
Reverse engineering teaches more than textbooks

Studying real vehicles — measuring, photographing, and questioning every design decision — gave me context that no lecture ever could. Understanding why existing dune buggies made specific geometry choices made my own decisions more deliberate and defensible.

03
Parallel workstreams are the only way to hit tight deadlines

One month for a complete vehicle design forced me to run CATIA ergonomics, Lotus Shark suspension iterations, and SolidWorks assembly simultaneously — not sequentially. Learning to keep partial models consistent across tools while each was still evolving was the hardest and most valuable skill I took away.

04
Data-driven down-selection beats aesthetic preference

Presenting two distinct roll cage configurations allowed for a rigorous comparative analysis of load path efficiency. By evaluating Iteration A against Iteration B, the team could objectively select the design that offered the highest structural rigidity with the lowest material consumption. This taught me that the "best" design isn't the most complex one, but the one that achieves the safety factor with the most elegant and manufacturable load path.
